planting.note: Direct sow 12–16 weeks before first fall frost so roots mature in cool fall weather; in zones 3–7 sow June–July. Rutabaga requires a long, cool growing season — unlike turnips, which can be harvested young in 50–60 days, rutabagas need the full 85–95 days to develop their full size and flavor. Frost dramatically improves rutabaga flavor by converting starches to sugars — leave roots in the ground through hard frosts and harvest in late fall or winter. Thin to 6–8 inches apart. In zone 9 the late-fall/winter growing season is too short for full rutabaga development — zones 3–7 are the primary growing regions.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, loose, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, stone-free soil; Laurentian is the standard North American rutabaga variety — a natural hybrid between turnip and cabbage producing large, round to slightly elongated roots with the characteristic purple shoulder above ground and yellow-tan below, with golden-yellow, dense, starchy flesh distinctly different from the white flesh of turnips. Rutabaga (called 'swede' in British English, reflecting its Swedish origin) has a richer, more complex flavor than turnip — the yellow flesh is denser, starchier, and more nutrionally robust with a sweeter, more earthy, complex flavor that becomes significantly more intense and sweet after hard frost exposure. The classic rutabaga preparations — mashed ('neeps' in Scotland, traditionally served with haggis), chunked in beef stew, roasted with butter and herbs, or pureed into soups — take full advantage of the dense, deeply flavored flesh that holds its shape better than potato while contributing its own distinctive sweetness and earthiness. Laurentian's large size (roots can reach softball size in good conditions), excellent storage quality (roots keep for months in cool storage), and consistent purple-top appearance make it the most reliable and widely grown rutabaga for home and market garden production.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Rutabaga Production Guide
University of Minnesota Extension — Root Vegetable Growing in Northern Climates
University of Wisconsin Extension — Rutabaga and Turnip Production

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Graffiti pentas is grown as an annual in zones below 10 and as a short-lived perennial in zone 10–11 where light frosts are rare. Deadheading spent flower clusters encourages continuous bloom but is not strictly necessary — Graffiti series was selected for its tendency to bloom continuously without requiring deadheading. In zone 9 pentas thrives in the hottest summer weeks when most other flowering annuals decline, blooming most freely from June through October in intense heat.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Graffiti Red is an All-America Selections award-winning compact pentas (Egyptian star flower) from the Graffiti series — a heat-loving tropical annual producing dense, domed clusters of vivid red, five-petaled star-shaped flowers continuously from planting until frost. Pentas is genuinely tropical in its heat preferences, performing most vigorously in zone 9's intense summer months and essentially thriving in the conditions (high temperature, high humidity) that cause most cool-season annuals to fail. The five-petaled star flowers are a key butterfly, hummingbird, and skipper nectar source — the flat, open flower structure accessible to a wide range of pollinators and the brilliant red color attracting hummingbirds make Graffiti Red one of the most effective wildlife garden plants for the Gulf Coast summer. The compact Graffiti habit (12–18 inches vs. the sprawling 3+ feet of older pentas varieties) makes it suitable for container plantings, front-border use, and formal bedding where tidiness is required.
sources: All-America Selections — Pentas Graffiti Red Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pentas for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Summer Annual Flowers for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Serenita Angelonia is sold primarily as transplants rather than direct seeded — seed germination is reliable indoors but the long growing season before bloom makes direct seeding impractical. In zone 9 angelonia performs magnificently in summer heat, blooming prolifically from spring through fall frost without the heat-related decline seen in snapdragons and other cool-season spike flowers. Deadheading is not necessary — plants are self-cleaning and bloom continuously without intervention.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — angelonia is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and performs well in the lean, dry summer conditions of zone 9; Serenita Pink is a heat-loving annual from the Serenita series of compact angelonia (summer snapdragon), producing upright spikes of soft pink, two-lipped flowers that closely resemble miniature snapdragon blooms while performing in summer heat conditions that cause true snapdragons to fail completely. Angelonia is one of the very best summer annuals for zone 9 and the Gulf South precisely because it thrives rather than merely survives in high heat and humidity — blooming continuously from late spring through frost in the conditions that challenge most other annual flowers. The soft pink flowers with their delicate, slightly grape-scented fragrance (angelonia has a subtle sweet scent that intensifies in warm weather) add a refined, airy texture to summer garden beds through their vertical spike form and light color. The Serenita series was selected for compact habit, early flowering, and excellent branching — producing well-branched, tidy plants that do not require pinching or deadheading to maintain a full, floriferous appearance through the entire season.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Angelonia for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Summer Annual Flowers for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant tubers after all frost danger has passed when soil temperature is consistently above 65°F; in zone 9 plant April–June. Florida Moonlight is a sun-tolerant caladium variety — it handles 4–6 hours of direct sun better than most caladiums, making it usable in brighter locations than typical deep-shade caladiums, though it performs best with afternoon shade protection. In zone 9 caladium tubers can remain in the ground year-round in the warmest areas; in cooler zone 9 locations dig and store tubers after the foliage dies back in fall. The white leaves actually brighten shaded areas dramatically, making Florida Moonlight one of the best choices for illuminating dark garden corners.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil rich in organic matter — caladiums are woodland tropical plants that require the consistent moisture and filtered light of their forest-floor habitat; Florida Moonlight is a strap-leaf caladium variety producing medium-sized, lance-shaped leaves that are an exceptional, nearly pure white with thin, barely visible veins and delicate green leaf margins — one of the brightest and most luminous white caladiums available, producing a striking glow-in-the-shade effect that makes shaded areas appear lit from within. White caladiums like Florida Moonlight are specifically valued for their ability to brighten and visually enlarge dark garden areas — the reflective white leaves catch and amplify whatever ambient light reaches the shade garden, creating an appearance of much more light than is actually present. Florida Moonlight is also one of the more sun-tolerant caladiums, accepting 4–6 hours of direct morning sun with good moisture — making it more versatile than shade-only varieties for partially shaded transitional garden areas. In zone 9's hot summers, the combination of tropical heat tolerance and shade preference makes Florida Moonlight one of the most practical ornamental plants for the deep shade under mature trees where few other plants thrive.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Caladium Production and Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Caladiums for Gulf South Shade Gardens
Florida Agricultural Experiment Station — Caladium Variety Evaluation

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–June. Sundial series portulaca flowers stay open later into the afternoon than older varieties (which close in overcast or afternoon conditions), making them more useful as a continuous-color bedding plant throughout the day. In zone 9 portulaca thrives in the hottest, driest summer conditions — a perfect full-sun annual for hot, dry slopes, rock gardens, and containers where irrigation is minimal. Sow seeds thinly on the surface of the soil (do not cover — seeds require light to germinate) and mist gently until seedlings establish.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, sandy or gritty, neutral to slightly acidic soil — portulaca demands excellent drainage and actually blooms best in poor, dry soil, languishing in rich or wet conditions; Sundial Pink is a large-flowered (1.5-inch) semi-double portulaca (moss rose) with bright cerise-pink blooms from the Sundial series, which was specifically developed to solve portulaca's traditional failing: the original moss roses closed their flowers in afternoon heat, overcast conditions, and late in the day, leaving the plant bloomless for much of the day. The Sundial series flowers stay open significantly longer than older varieties — an important improvement for a plant intended to provide continuous color in hot, sunny garden locations throughout the day. The succulent, needle-like leaves store water in their tissues, giving portulaca remarkable drought tolerance that allows it to survive periods of complete neglect and to thrive in the kind of hot, dry, reflected-heat locations (paved beds, rocky slopes, container walls) that would kill almost any other flowering annual. In zone 9's brutal summer heat, portulaca is among the very few flowering plants that genuinely improves in performance as temperatures rise.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Portulaca for Florida Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Drought-Tolerant Annual Flowers
